name: Pinesque and Balsa de Larralde - round trip from Parque Lineal de Plaza
name_original: Pinesque y Balsa de Larralde — ida y vuelta desde Parque Lineal de Plaza
region_intro: Road cycling around Zaragoza offers diverse landscapes, from the flat plains along the Ebro River to the arid terrain of the Monegros Desert. The region features extensive cycling infrastructure, including dedicated paths along the Ebro and its tributaries. Cyclists can explore varied topography, including gentle river routes and more challenging ascents into surrounding hills. This area provides a range of road cycling experiences across different elevations and environments. Best road cycling routes around Zaragoza The most popular road cycling route is Virgen de la Dorleta Memorial Loop from Zaragoza, a 40.2 miles (64.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 59 minutes to complete. This moderate route features a memorial landmark and varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Murales de Alfamén, Parque de Muel y Santuario de la Virgen de las Lagunas — round trip from Zaragoza, a moderate 52.1 miles (83.8 km) path. This route offers scenic views through parks and past a sanctuary. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Alcubierre Loop from Zaragoza, a 54.4 miles (87.5 km) trail leading through the Monegros landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 44 minutes. Road cycling around Zaragoza is defined by routes along the Ebro River, through the Monegros Desert, and into surrounding hills. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Zaragoza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 200 reviews. More than 9,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Zaragoza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Zaragoza?
A: There are over 130 road cycling routes around Zaragoza, offering a wide range of experiences for cyclists of all levels. These include 38 easy, 73 moderate, and 26 difficult routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Zaragoza?
A: Zaragoza offers diverse terrain for road cycling. You can expect flat plains along the Ebro River, arid landscapes in the Monegros Desert, and routes with more challenging ascents into the surrounding hills. The region provides a mix of gentle river routes and varied topography.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988435650/hermitage-of-the-virgin-of-the-eagle-and-las-torcas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle and Las Torcas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain, covering over 145 km with more than 1500 meters of climbing. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988448558/zaragoza-herrera-de-los-navarros-zaragoza-circular-by-fuendetodos-and-virgen-de-la-doloreta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zaragoza - Herrera de los Navarros - Zaragoza circular by Fuendetodos and Virgen de la Doloreta&lt;/a&gt;, which is nearly 150 km long with over 1400 meters of ascent.

Q: What landmarks or natural features can I see along road cycling routes in Zaragoza?
A: Many routes offer scenic views and pass by interesting landmarks. You might encounter viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/94970" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de las Planas&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6575360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de las Zorras&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992661818/virgen-de-la-dorleta-memorial-loop-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Dorleta Memorial Loop from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; passes a significant memorial. The region also features natural monuments such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojo de las Almunias&lt;/a&gt; and the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2834036" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Zafrane&lt;/a&gt; gorge.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Zaragoza?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Zaragoza are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988428972/zuera-circular-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zuera - circular from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991232800/quinto-codo-belchite-roden-circular-from-fuentes-del-ebro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quinto - Codo - Belchite - Rodén — circular from Fuentes del Ebro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Zaragoza?
A: Spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for road cycling in Zaragoza, offering pleasant temperatures. Summers can be very hot, especially in the Monegros Desert, and the 'Cierzo' wind can be strong at times, particularly in winter and spring. Checking the weather forecast for wind conditions is advisable.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes in Zaragoza?
A: Zaragoza offers several easy and moderate routes suitable for families. The extensive network of bike lanes along the Ebro River, including sections of the Ebro Nature Trail (GR 99) and the 'Anillo Verde de Zaragoza' (Green Belt), provides relatively flat and safe options for family outings.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Zaragoza?
A: The road cycling routes in Zaragoza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 200 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from river plains to semi-deserts, and the variety of routes catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Can I find bike rentals or repair services in Zaragoza?
A: Yes, Zaragoza is a cyclist-friendly city. 'La Cicleria' is a well-known local hub that offers bike rentals, repairs, and information on local routes, making it convenient for visitors to access cycling services.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through the Monegros Desert?
A: Yes, the Monegros Desert is a unique and popular area for cycling near Zaragoza. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988459338/puerto-de-alcubierre-loop-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Alcubierre Loop from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; is a notable route that takes you through these distinct arid landscapes of stone and sand.

Q: Are there any routes that feature parks or sanctuaries?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989987141/murales-de-alfamen-parque-de-muel-y-santuario-de-la-virgen-de-las-lagunas-round-trip-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Murales de Alfamén, Parque de Muel y Santuario de la Virgen de las Lagunas — round trip from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate route that offers scenic views through parks and past a sanctuary.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route available from Zaragoza?
A: One of the longest routes is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988448558/zaragoza-herrera-de-los-navarros-zaragoza-circular-by-fuendetodos-and-virgen-de-la-doloreta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zaragoza - Herrera de los Navarros - Zaragoza circular by Fuendetodos and Virgen de la Doloreta&lt;/a&gt;, which spans nearly 150 km. Another extensive option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988435650/hermitage-of-the-virgin-of-the-eagle-and-las-torcas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle and Las Torcas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering over 145 km.
tips:
La Balsa de Larralde or Torre Medina is a wetland located in the municipality of Zaragoza, in the neighborhood of Torre Medina, belonging to the rural neighborhood of Garrapinillos. This pond, together with the Ojo del Cura and El Fraile, are part of an old set of lagoons that surround the neighborhoods of Casetas and Garrapinillos and that were formed by the subsidence of the land since the subsoil plasters were diluted by groundwater. and they end up collapsing forming sinkholes.

La Balsa de Larralde, recovered by the Ebro Hydrographic Confederation (CHE) and by the Aragon Naturalist Association, is a 4-hectare wetland with a great and rich variety of flora and fauna. It has a maximum depth of 5 meters.

La Balsa is surrounded by poplars and reedbeds. The latter forms a belt that protects the raft as aquatic vegetation. Fundamentally it is formed by two species of plants: the reed itself and the cattail. This environment is used by a diversity of animals to take refuge, search for food or build their nests.

Parque Lineal de Plaza, a nice area to enjoy nature near Zaragoza. Different paths will lead us to a large lake inhabited by various species of birds such as ducks or swans or to the Solar Labyrinth or the Baroque Labyrinth

waypoints.name: Sunset View Over the Lake, Parque Lineal de Plaza
